---
title: "project4_report"
author: "Tianyu Cao"
subtitle: "STATS 220 Semester One 2026"

output:
  html_document:
    code_folding: hide
---
```{css}
body {
  background-color: #efedf5;
}

h2 {
  color: #756bb1;
}

p {
  font-size: 16px;
}
```

## **Introduction**

Project 4 uses the Google Form I designed in Project 2 to collect data primarily focused on users’ behavioral responses to advertising content on social media platforms. It examines the reasons users click on ads they see while using the software, whether they recognize them as ads, subsequent purchasing behavior, product prices, and other related information, while also tracking how these behaviors change over time.

I collected all of this data myself. When creating the data visualizations, I focused on the reasons why users clicked on ads, the relationship between price and purchasing behavior, and how users interacted with ads at different times.

I chose to use bar charts to display the frequencies of categorical variables because this type of chart clearly illustrates the differences in the number of entries across different categories; I used box plots to analyze price distributions because they effectively show the price ranges and medians across different groups; and I used time-series charts to analyze users’ active times throughout the day.

During the initial data processing phase, I attempted to create charts directly using the raw open-ended responses, but the content was too scattered, resulting in unclear charts. Therefore, I used `case_when()` and `str_detect()` to categorize similar responses, making the results easier to interpret and analyze.


## **Visual data story**

My visual data story goes beyond simply presenting charts; it also attempts to construct a complete data narrative by highlighting the connections between different charts. For example, I begin by analyzing why users click on ads, then explore the relationship between price and purchasing behavior, and finally use time-series data to analyze users’ ad interaction patterns throughout the day.

In terms of visual design, I used a consistent color scheme and organized the content through HTML page structure and headings to make the entire visual data story more coherent and readable.

My visual data story webpage
https://github.com/tianyucaocao/stats220/commits?author=tianyucaocao


## **Learning reflection**

Through Module 4, I learned that data visualization is not just about “drawing charts”; more importantly, it’s about how to convey information to readers through charts. Different types of charts are suitable for presenting different types of data, so when selecting a chart, it is necessary to consider the data type and the key points you want to convey.

In this project, I also realized the importance of data cleaning. Especially when dealing with open-ended text responses, if the data is not categorized and organized, it becomes very chaotic and difficult to analyze. Consequently, I learned how to use functions such as `mutate()`, `case_when()`, and `str_detect()` to process the data.

In the future, I hope to further my studies in data-related technologies, such as interactive data visualization, web data analysis, and how to use more advanced tools to create dynamic dashboards. I believe these skills can be applied to real-world business analysis and user behavior research.


## **Appendix**

```{r file='data_visualisation.R', eval=FALSE, echo=TRUE}

```
